module-2-department-participation-scorec.label: Module 2 Project: Department Participation Scorecard
module-2-department-participation-scorec.summary: Build an all-department scorecard with course, enrollment, and distinct-student counts in six cumulative steps.

sketch0.title: A Scorecard That Includes Quiet Departments

step1_preserve_departments.title: Preserve Every Department
step1_preserve_departments.prompt: Start the module project. In step 1 of 6, Write a query to list all departments, ensuring that even those without courses are included. Use a LEFT JOIN to connect departments with courses.
step1_preserve_departments.hint: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

step2_count_courses.title: Count Courses Correctly
step2_count_courses.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 2 of 6, Modify your query to count the number of courses per department, ensuring that departments with no courses show a count of zero.
step2_count_courses.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.
help.hint_2: Run the previous working code first, then make only the focused change for this step.

step2_count_courses.starterCode:
SELECT d.name, c.title
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id;
-- Project step 2: Count Courses Correctly
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Modify your query to count the number of courses per department, ensuring that departments with no courses show a count of zero
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step2_count_courses.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
GROUP BY d.name;

main_py.content:
SELECT d.name, c.title
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id;
-- Project step 2: Count Courses Correctly
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Modify your query to count the number of courses per department, ensuring that departments with no courses show a count of zero
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
GROUP BY d.name;

step3_extend_enrollments.title: Extend to Enrollment Records
step3_extend_enrollments.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 3 of 6, Extend your query to include enrollments, ensuring all departments are preserved. Use a LEFT JOIN to connect courses with enrollments.
step3_extend_enrollments.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

step3_extend_enrollments.starterCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 3: Extend to Enrollment Records
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Extend your query to include enrollments, ensuring all departments are preserved
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step3_extend_enrollments.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count

GROUP BY d.name;

main_py.content:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
GROUP BY d.name;

FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;

step4_count_enrollments.title: Count Enrollment Rows
step4_count_enrollments.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 4 of 6, Add a count of enrollment rows per department to your query, ensuring the department grain is maintained.
step4_count_enrollments.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.
help.hint_2: Run the previous working code first, then make only the focused change for this step.

step4_count_enrollments.starterCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 4: Count Enrollment Rows
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Add a count of enrollment rows per department to your query, ensuring the department grain is maintained
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step4_count_enrollments.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;

main_py.content:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 4: Count Enrollment Rows
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Add a count of enrollment rows per department to your query, ensuring the department grain is maintained
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;

step5_count_unique_students.title: Count Unique Students
step5_count_unique_students.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 5 of 6, Modify your query to count unique students per department, ensuring each student is counted only once per department.
step5_count_unique_students.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.
help.hint_2: Run the previous working code first, then make only the focused change for this step.

step5_count_unique_students.starterCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 5: Count Unique Students
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Modify your query to count unique students per department, ensuring each student is counted only once per department
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step5_count_unique_students.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count, COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS student_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;

main_py.content:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 5: Count Unique Students
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Modify your query to count unique students per department, ensuring each student is counted only once per department
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count, COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS student_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;

step6_rank_scorecard.title: Rank the Scorecard Predictably
step6_rank_scorecard.prompt: Continue the same module project from the previous working step. In step 6 of 6, Sort the final scorecard by enrollment count descending, then by department name ascending.
step6_rank_scorecard.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: This module project is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.

step6_rank_scorecard.starterCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count, COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS student_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 6: Rank the Scorecard Predictably
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Sort the final scorecard by enrollment count descending, then by department name ascending
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step6_rank_scorecard.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count, COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS student_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name
ORDER BY enrollment_count DESC, d.name ASC;

main_py.content:
SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count, COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS student_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name;
-- Project step 6: Rank the Scorecard Predictably
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Sort the final scorecard by enrollment count descending, then by department name ascending
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT d.name, COUNT(DISTINCT c.id) AS course_count, COUNT(e.id) AS enrollment_count, COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS student_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN courses c ON d.id = c.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments e ON c.id = e.course_id
GROUP BY d.name
ORDER BY enrollment_count DESC, d.name ASC;

sketch-intro.title: Project brief: department participation scorecard
sketch-intro.bodyMarkdown:
School leaders need a scorecard that includes every department—not only the departments with active courses and enrollments. Keep **one row per department** from the beginning of the query to the end. The Arts department must remain visible even when its related counts are zero.

Your report will travel from `departments` through `courses` to `enrollments`. It will measure three different things: distinct courses, enrollment rows, and distinct participating students. Those metrics share a join path but not the same counting rule. A course or student may repeat after the joins, while an enrollment row is already the event being counted.

Build the scorecard in six cumulative steps. First preserve every department. Then introduce each relationship and metric without losing the department grain. Finish with deterministic ranking so the report is stable when two departments share the same count.

A trustworthy scorecard makes its logic visible: the left joins explain why quiet departments survive, the counted identifiers explain what each number measures, and the grouping explains why the result remains one row per department.
